content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Darrell W. Larsen 
County Counsel 
County of sutter 
463 Second Street 
Yuba City, CA 
95991 
Dear Mr. Larsen: 
May 16, 1986 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-86-l27 
Thank you for your letter requesting advice on behalf of 
Sutter County Supervisor Allen Eager concerning his duties 
under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act.1I 
This letter confirms the advice I provided to 
you By telephone on April 29, 1986, and the advice I provided 
to Supervisor Eager by telephone on May 13, 1986. 
This advice 
is based on the facts you provided in your letter and 
subsequent telephone conversations. 
FACTS 
The State Department of Corrections (the "Department") has 
identified two areas in sutter County, and one area in Yuba 
County, as being potentially suitable for the placement of a 
state prison. 
The Legislature has allocated funds to conduct a 
feasibility study of these three areas. 
One of these areas includes real property owned by 
Supervisor Eager. 
This property is currently farmland. 
The 
neighboring property owners have contacted the Department to 
indicate their interest in selling their property to the state 
for a prison site. 
A representative of the Department has 
visited Supervisor Eager's property and has stated that the 
Supervisor's property, along with one or more neighboring 
parcels, might be an appropriate location for the prison. 
However, the Department has yet to identify any specific site 
as the probable location for the future prison. 
The Board of supervisors, without Supervisor Eager's 
participation, has decided to send a negative comment to the 
11 
Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 


Darrell W. Larsen 
May 16, 1986 
Page 2 
Department regarding the siting of any prison in sutter 
county. 
A number of residents of Sutter county disagree with 
the decision to send the negative comment, and are interested 
in proposing a ballot measure which would support the location 
of a prison in sutter county. 
QUESTION 
You have asked whether supervisor Eager may do any of the 
following: 
(1) 
Sign an initiative petition which has as its object 
the request to the Department from Sutter County that the 
feasibility study be continued. 
(2) 
Sign an initiative petition which has as its object a 
statement that the citizens of Sutter County would welcome the 
placement of a prison within the County. 
(3) 
Circulate the initiative petitions described in (1) 
and (2) above. 
(4) 
Author letters in favor of placement of a prison 
within sutter County. 
(5) 
Join citizens' committees organized for the purpose of 
promoting placement of a prison within sutter county. 
(6) 
Speak at meetings, other than those of the Board of 
Supervisors or other governmental agencies, with regard to the 
desirability of placement of a prison within the County. 
(7) 
Speak at meetings of the Board of Supervisors or other 
governmental bodies with regard to the advisability of 
placement of a prison within sutter County so long as he does 
so in his individual capacity and not as a county supervisor. 
(8) 
Make a motion or vote on a motion to place an advisory 
measure on the November ballot which would give the voters of 
Sutter County the opportunity to express their interest in 
having a prison located in sutter County. 
CONCLUSION 
Supervisor Eager must disqualify himself from making, 
participating in, or using his official position to influence 
the decision of the Board of Supervisors concerning the 
placement of a prison in sutter County so long as his property 
is within an area designated as a potential location for a 
prison, or is included in or is near the specific site of a 


Darrell W. Larsen 
May 16, 1986 
Page 3 
proposed prison. 
Accordingly, he may not make a motion or vote 
on a motion to place the advisory measure described in (8), 
above, on the ballot. 
However, Supervisor Eager may perform 
any of the actions in (1) through (7), above. 
However, if he 
communicates with a government agency on the subject of the 
prison, he must act only in his individual capacity and take 
care that he does not represent, or purport to represent that 
his views are the views of the Board of Supervisors, or use his 
official stationery for those communications. 
ANALYSIS 
section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, 
participating in, or using his official position to influence 
any governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to 
know he has a financial interest. 
A public official has a 
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally, on any real property in which the official has a 
direct or indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) 
or more. 
Section 87l03(b). 
Supervisor Eager has an interest in real property that 
presumably is worth at least $1,000. 
This property is included 
in one of the general areas designated by the Department of 
Corrections as a potential prison site. 
If a prison is located 
on or near Supervisor Eager's property, we think it would 
materially affect the use and enjoyment of that property, and 
thus the fair market value of the property. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Sections l8702(b) (2) and l8702.l(a) (3) (copies enclosed). 
Accordingly, we conclude that decisions of the Board of 
Supervisors which are intended to influence the State's 
decision about locating a prison in Sutter County would have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Supervisor 
Eager's property. 
Thus, Supervisor Eager must disqualify 
himself from making, participating in, or using his official 
position to influence the decision concerning the prison. 
Supervisor Eager must disqualify himself until such time as the 
Department has decided that it will not locate a prison on or 
near Supervisor Eager's property.~ 
~ I have enclosed a copy of the Thorner Opinion, 1 FPPC 
198 (No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 1975), which discusses the issue of 
foreseeability. 
In our opinion, Supervisor Eager's position is 
analogous to that of an individual that has bid on a contract, 
and is then faced with a decision which would affect the 
contract. 


Darrell W. Larsen 
May 16, 1986 
Page 4 
The restrictions on Supervisor Eager's actions apply with 
respect to any governmental decisions, whether or not the Board 
of supervisors is the final decisionmaker. 
In this case, the 
Board of Supervisors clearly does not have the authority to 
make the final decision about locating a prison in sutter 
County. 
Nevertheless, a resolution from the Board of 
Supervisors to another government agency would be an attempt to 
influence a governmental decision. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code section 
18700.1(c) (copy enclosed). 
This conclusion is in accord with 
our previous advice in similar situations. 
Advice Letter to 
Jon A. Blegan, No. A-85-176; Advice Letter to Scott R. Keene, 
No. A-81-512 (copies enclosed). 
You have advised us that the Board of Supervisors has 
already acted to oppose the location of a prison in Sutter 
County. 
We have been informed by a representative of the 
Department that under those circumstances, there is little hope 
of the Department deciding to locate a prison in Sutter County, 
and the Department has halted the feasibility study. 
However, 
if the position of the Board of Supervisors could be reversed, 
or even if an advisory measure were to qualify for the November 
ballot, the Department would resume the feasibility study and 
the chances that the Department would locate a prison in sutter 
County would be significantly increased. 
These facts indicate that the decision to place an advisory 
measure on the ballot would have the effect of permitting an 
action to go forward which would otherwise be blocked. 
Thus, 
because the decision to place an advisory measure on the ballot 
would significantly increase the chances of a prison being 
located in Sutter County, Supervisor Eager must refrain from 
participating in any way in the Board of Supervisors' decisoin 
regarding the advisory ballot measure. 
Our conclusion might be 
different if the Board of Supervisors had not already taken a 
position on the prison. 
In that case, because the Department 
would not have already halted the feasibility study in Sutter 
County, we think the mere decision to place the issue before 
the voters would have a more remote effect on Supervisor 
Eager's property. 
However, Supervisor Eager would still be 
prohibited from participating in the final decision of the 
Board of Supervisors, after the advisory election, concerning 
the County's attitude toward siting a prison in sutter County. 
Although Supervisor Eager has a conflict of interest with 
respect to the prison decisions, the Political Reform Act does 
not prevent him from communicating with the general public or 
the press concerning his views on the prison decision. 
2 Cal. 
Adm. Code section 18700.1(b) (2). 
Furthermore, Supervisor 
Eager, is not prohibited from signing or circulating petitions, 


Darrell W. Larsen 
May 16, 1986 
Page 5 
as an individual, which are intended to influence the decision 
of the Board of Supervisors on this matter. 
In addition, 
Supervisor Eager may address the Board of Supervisors or other 
governmental agencies regarding the prison issue in the same 
manner as a member of the general public, solely to represent 
his personal interests. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700.1(b) (1). It is important to note that Supervisor Eager 
may not act as the representative of any other persons before 
the Board of Supervisors or any agency subject to the 
appointive or budgetary control of the Board of Supervisors, 
and he may not communicate privately with members of the Board 
of Supervisors concerning this issue. 
Finally, Supervisor 
Eager may author letters concerning the prison issue, so long 
as he does so in his individual capacity and does not use 
official stationery or purport to represent the views of the 
Board of Supervisors. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700.1(c). 
I have advised you that this advice is limited to the 
interpretation of the Political Reform Act; I have also 
suggested that you should consider whether Government Code 
Section 1090 would further limit Supervisor Eager's activities. 
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, 
please contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED:p1h 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 
cf!att1/v~ f 
&VLM;7L~. 
Kathryn ~. Donovan 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


DARRELL W. LARSEN 
County Counsel 
OFFICE OF THE COUNTY COUNSEL 
COUNTY OF strrrER 
463 SECOND STREET 
YUBA CITY, CALIFORNIA 95991 
Ms. Kathy Donovan 
Attorney at Law 
Fair political Practices 
Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
RE: 
CONFLICT OF INTEREST 
Dear Ms. Donovan: 
PHONE ~~X~ 741-7110 
April 15, 1986 
RONALD S. ERICKSON 
Deputy County Counsel 
JA:vtES SCANLON 
Deputy County Counsel 
As we discussed via telephone on April 15, 1986, this office is 
seeking some guidance with regard to restrictions on the activities 
of a county supervisor who is a landowner in the county in which 
the State Department of Corrections has expressed an interest in 
constructing a prison. 
The State Department of Corrections has 
identified two sites in Sutter County and one in our neighboring 
county Yuba, as being potentially suitable for the placement of a 
state prison. 
Monies have been allocated by the state Legislature 
IDconduct a feasibility study of these three sites. 
In addition, 
some property owners have contacted the State Department of Correc-
tions indicating that they might be willing to sell their property 
to the State for a prison site. 
Included within this group are one 
or two neighbors of a county supervisor. 
A representative of the State Department of Corrections has visited 
the supervisor's property and has stated that the property of the 
supervisor along with one or more of his neighbors might be an 
appropriate location for a prison. 
However, this is no more than a 
possibility. 
The Department of Corrections continues to be primarily 
interested in the three sites which were to be the subject of a 
feasibility study. 
The supervisor's property would only be considered 
if all three sites were rejected. 
Further, the supervisor's property 
may not be the only "fall back" property that might be considered by 
the Department of Corrections if the first three sites turn out to be 
unacceptable. 
The Board of Supervisors, with the subject supervisor not partiCipat-
ing in either the discussion or the vote, has determined to send a 
negative comment to the Department of Corrections with regard to 
siting any prison within this county. 
A number of people do not 
agree with that determination as they believe the location of a prison 
in thS county would reap significant financial benefits, locally. 
There is therefore an interest in placing the proposal before the 
voters as a ballot measure. 
We would appreciate your guidance with 
regard to what role the supervisor who owns property which has been 


Ms. Kathy Donovan 
April 15, 1986 
Page Two 
visited by the State Department of Corrections may play in further 
consideration of this matter. 
1. 
May the supervisor sign an initiative petition having as its 
object the request to the State Department of Corrections from 
Sutter County that the feasibility study be continued? 
Please 
recall that the three properties currently being considered by 
the Department of Corrections do not include any property of 
the supervisor. 
2. 
May the supervisor sign an initiative petition which has as its 
object not the continuation of a feasibility study, but rather 
a statement that the citizens of Sutter County would welcome the 
placement of a prison within this county? 
3. 
With regard to the preceding two questions, may the supervisor, 
in addition to signing the petition, circulate such a petition, 
in each case? 
4. 
May the supervisor author letters in favor of placement of a pri-
son within Sutter County. 
5. 
May the supervisor join citizens' committees organized for the 
purpose of promoting the placement of a prison within Sutter 
County? 
6. 
May the supervisor speak at public meetings, other than those of 
the Board of Supervisors and other government bodies, if his 
speech is going to to touch on the desirability of locating a 
state prison within this county? 
7. 
May the supervisor speak at meetings of the Board of Supervisors 
or other governmental bodies with regard to the advisability of 
placement of a prison within Sutter County so long as he does 
SO in his individual capacity and not as a county supervisor? 
Your courtesies and assistance have been appreciated. 
If at all possible, 
we would request a response to so many of these questions as you can 
answer by April 29, 1986. 
Very truly yours, 
judfJf/~~ 
DARRELL W. L~;~ 
SUTTER COUNTY COUNSEL 
DWL:js
